era_context: The Abbasid Caliphate under al-Ma'mun is in its final years; the caliph is aging and his health is declining. The Carolingian Empire is in the midst of civil war over succession. The Tang Dynasty continues its gradual decline with increasing regional fragmentation. Viking raids on Western Europe are intensifying. In the Islamic world, the Mu'tazilite theological movement is at its height of influence under al-Ma'mun's patronage.

events.title: Abbasid Caliph al-Ma'mun's final military campaigns against Byzantine Empire
events.region: Abbasid Caliphate and Byzantine Empire (Levant, Anatolia)
events.description: Al-Ma'mun, now in his final years, continues military campaigns against the Byzantine Empire in Anatolia and the Levant. These campaigns are part of the broader pattern of Abbasid-Byzantine conflict that has characterised the 9th century. Al-Ma'mun's military strategy emphasises the use of professional armies and fortified positions rather than the large-scale raids of earlier periods. The exact details of campaigns in 832 are not always clearly documented, but the pattern of Abbasid military activity is evident from contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

sources.name: al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings, 9th–10th century

events.certainty_note: Al-Ma'mun's military campaigns in 832 are attested in Islamic historiography, but specific details and dates may be conflated or misdated in later compilation.
cross_references: Part of broader Abbasid-Byzantine conflict in 9th century

events.title: Carolingian civil war: Louis the Pious attempts to restore his authority; renewed conflict with sons
events.description: Following the Battle of Fontenoy-en-Puisaye in 833 (which occurred after 832), Louis the Pious attempts to restore his authority in 832. The year 832 sees continued political instability and military manoeuvring as Louis and his sons contest the division of the empire. The exact chronology of events in 832 is somewhat unclear from surviving sources, but the pattern of conflict is evident.
key_figures: Louis the German (future king of East Francia)
Charles the Bald (future king of West Francia)

sources.name: Astronomer, Life of Louis the Pious, 9th century

events.certainty_note: The Carolingian succession crisis of 832 is attested in Frankish chronicles, but the specific events and chronology of 832 are somewhat unclear due to the complexity of the conflict and the nature of the sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Carolingian succession crisis (830–843)

events.title: Viking raids continue on English and Frankish coasts
events.description: Viking raids continue to plague English and Frankish coastal regions in 832, as part of the sustained Norse expansion into Western Europe during the 830s. The exact targets and scale of individual raids in 832 are not always clearly documented, but the pattern of escalating Norse activity is evident from contemporary and near-contemporary sources.
events.certainty_note: Viking activity in 832 is attested in the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle and Frankish sources, but specific raids may be conflated or misdated in later compilation.
cross_references: Part of broader Viking Age expansion in 9th century

events.title: Tang Dynasty: Wenzong's reign continues; internal factional conflict persists
events.description: Emperor Wenzong of Tang (r. 827–840) continues to face factional conflict between eunuchs and scholar-officials (literati) within the imperial court. The year 832 sees continued political instability as these factions compete for influence. This internal conflict contributes to the broader decline of Tang central authority.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled by Sima Guang, 11th century
Old Book of Tang (Jiu Tangshu), 10th century

events.certainty_note: The factional conflicts of Wenzong's reign are well-documented in later Chinese historiography, but precise dating of individual episodes to 832 is conventional.

disconfirming_evidence: The chronology of the Carolingian civil war in 832 is somewhat unclear from surviving sources; some scholars place key events in 831 or 833 rather than 832. The exact nature of al-Ma'mun's military campaigns in 832 is not well-documented, and later sources may conflate campaigns from different years.
historiographic_note: The year 832 is moderately well-documented in Frankish and Islamic sources, though both are filtered through later compilation. The Frankish sources on the civil war are relatively abundant but often reflect the perspective of the victorious sons. Islamic sources on al-Ma'mun are generally reliable but written decades or centuries after the events. Chinese sources on the Tang Dynasty are abundant but reflect the perspective of the literati class. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are absent from contemporary written records.

graph_edges.note: Al-Ma'mun's final campaigns precede his death in 833
Political instability of 832 leads to Battle of Fontenoy-en-Puisaye in 833
Sustained pattern of Viking raids in early 830s
